rules.risk: Processes inside the container execute as UID 0. If the container shares the host user namespace, a breakout grants immediate host root access.
rules.remediation: Set a non-root user in the OCI runtime spec (process.user.uid) or use --user flag at container creation.

rules.risk: CAP_SYS_ADMIN enables mount, namespace, and cgroup operations that can bypass container isolation. Effectively equivalent to privileged mode.
rules.remediation: Remove CAP_SYS_ADMIN from the capability bounding set. Use fine-grained capabilities instead.

rules.description: Bind mounts exist that are not readonly
rules.risk: Writable bind mounts allow the container to modify host files. An attacker can tamper with host binaries, cron jobs, or configuration files.
rules.remediation: Add 'ro' to mount options for all bind mounts that do not require write access.

rules.risk: Without a seccomp profile, the container can invoke any system call. Dangerous syscalls (e.g., mount, ptrace, reboot) remain available to attackers.
rules.remediation: Apply a seccomp profile via --security-opt seccomp=<profile.json> or set seccompProfilePath in the OCI runtime spec.

rules.description: Systemd service unit does not set a non-root User directive
rules.risk: The container engine process runs as UID 0 on the host. Combined with a container escape, this yields immediate root access to the host.
rules.remediation: Add 'User=<non-root>' to the [Service] section of the systemd unit file.

rules.description: Readonly root filesystem is not enabled
rules.risk: An attacker who gains code execution inside the container can write to the root filesystem, enabling persistence or binary replacement.
rules.remediation: Set root.readonly to true in the OCI runtime spec or use --read-only flag.

rules.description: Container shares the host PID namespace
rules.risk: Host PID namespace sharing exposes all host processes to the container. An attacker can inspect, signal, or ptrace host processes, leading to credential theft or privilege escalation.
rules.remediation: Add a 'pid' namespace entry to linux.namespaces in the OCI runtime spec. Do not use --pid=host at container creation.

rules.description: Container shares the host network namespace
rules.risk: Host network namespace sharing gives the container full access to the host network stack, including listening on privileged ports, sniffing traffic, and bypassing network-level container isolation.
rules.remediation: Add a 'network' namespace entry to linux.namespaces in the OCI runtime spec. Do not use --network=host at container creation.

rules.description: Container shares the host IPC namespace
rules.risk: Host IPC namespace sharing allows the container to access shared memory segments on the host. An attacker can read or corrupt IPC resources used by host processes.
rules.remediation: Add an 'ipc' namespace entry to linux.namespaces in the OCI runtime spec. Do not use --ipc=host at container creation.

rules.risk: Capabilities such as CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_PTRACE, CAP_SYS_MODULE, CAP_NET_RAW, and CAP_NET_ADMIN can be exploited to escape container isolation, load kernel modules, sniff network traffic, or manipulate host namespaces.
rules.remediation: Remove dangerous capabilities from the bounding and effective sets. Use the minimum set of capabilities required by the workload.

rules.description: Container mounts a dangerous host path
rules.risk: Mounting sensitive host paths (/, /proc, /sys, /dev, /etc, container engine sockets) breaks container isolation and enables container escape, credential theft, or full host compromise.
rules.remediation: Remove bind mounts for sensitive paths. Use named volumes or tmpfs instead.

rules.description: noNewPrivileges is not enabled in the OCI runtime spec
rules.risk: Without noNewPrivileges, processes inside the container can gain more privileges than their parent via setuid binaries or kernel exploits.
rules.remediation: Set process.noNewPrivileges to true in the OCI runtime spec, or use --security-opt no-new-privileges at container creation.

rules.description: AppArmor profile is set to unconfined
rules.risk: Disabling AppArmor removes mandatory access control enforcement, allowing the container process unrestricted access to system resources within its namespace.
rules.remediation: Remove unconfined AppArmor profile. Use the default profile or provide a custom profile tailored to the workload.

rules.description: Bind mount uses shared or rshared propagation
rules.risk: Shared mount propagation allows mounts created inside the container to propagate to the host filesystem. An attacker can mount filesystems on the host from within the container, enabling container escape.
rules.remediation: Remove shared/rshared propagation from mount options. Use private or slave propagation instead.

rules.description: No memory limit configured in OCI runtime spec
rules.risk: Without a memory limit, a runaway process can consume all host memory, triggering the OOM killer against arbitrary host processes.
rules.remediation: Set linux.resources.memory.limit in the OCI runtime spec or use --memory flag at container creation.

rules.description: No PID limit configured in OCI runtime spec
rules.risk: Without a PID limit, a fork bomb inside the container can exhaust the host PID table, causing a system-wide denial of service.
rules.remediation: Set linux.resources.pids.limit in the OCI runtime spec or use --pids-limit flag.

rules.description: Critical kernel interfaces not masked (linux.maskedPaths incomplete)
rules.risk: Without masking /proc/kcore, /proc/sysrq-trigger, and similar interfaces, a container process can read kernel memory or trigger system reboots via sysrq.

rules.description: Container has no corresponding systemd service for automatic lifecycle management
rules.risk: In embedded/automotive environments all container services must be managed by systemd. A container without a systemd service will not auto-start at boot and has no supervised restart behavior.
rules.remediation: Create a systemd service unit file to manage the Podman container lifecycle (start, stop, restart).

rules.description: Systemd service does not restrict capabilities via CapabilityBoundingSet
rules.risk: Without CapabilityBoundingSet in the systemd unit, the Podman engine inherits all capabilities from its parent process, increasing the blast radius if the engine is compromised.
rules.remediation: Add 'CapabilityBoundingSet=' to the [Service] section of the systemd unit to restrict the engine to only the capabilities it requires.
